learning: When implementing managed one-shot continuations in Zig, run the first step on stack and only box state after the first real suspension to preserve a zero-allocation fast path.
evidence: zig build bench reported alloc_calls=0 for the no-capture loop and zig build run-effect-state plus zig build run-generator both passed in /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift
application: For future control-family features in shift, keep boxing behind the first suspension boundary and treat any no-capture allocation as a regression.

learning: When one-shot continuation wrappers can outlive their owner handle, prefer an explicit two-step Session lifecycle with close(mode) plus destroy() so teardown semantics stay safe without leaks or hidden global state.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the Session redesign passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench while preserving alloc_calls=0 in the no-capture loop.
application: For future shift ownership changes, separate semantic shutdown from memory reclamation and keep destroy() gated on drained active continuations.

learning: When a two-step Session lifecycle is explicit, prefer one strict cleanup helper that fails loudly on unexpected close or destroy states because swallowed lifecycle errors can hide ownership bugs in continuation runtimes.
evidence: During the Session redesign in /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, strict cleanup plus the final green suite (zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, zig build bench) replaced earlier silent catch-based teardown.
application: For future shift lifecycle changes, route cleanup through one strict helper and treat SessionBusy or SessionOpen during expected teardown as a bug unless the test is explicitly asserting it.

learning: When splitting lifecycle ownership in a Zig continuation runtime, prefer a strict close-then-destroy Session API because it keeps semantic shutdown separate from memory reclamation and preserves zero-allocation steady-state proofs.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the Session rewrite passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench with alloc_calls=0 in the benchmark loop.
application: For future shift ownership changes, keep close semantics explicit, make destroy() validate lifecycle state, and re-check the no-capture benchmark after any owner-model change.

learning: When post-destroy continuation handles must fail cleanly in Zig, prefer a separate continuation control block plus explicit clone()/release() because plain struct copies are otherwise untrackable.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the continuation-control redesign passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench while keeping alloc_calls=0 in the no-capture loop.
application: For future shift lifetime redesigns, move wrapper state out of the session owner and make extra handle references explicit instead of relying on invisible value copies.

learning: When hardening one-shot Zig continuation ownership, split the consuming owner handle from release-only aliases so active final release becomes impossible at the API surface and same-thread cleanup can be enforced.
evidence: zig build test passed after replacing clone/release with alias/release in src/control_spec.zig and src/raw.zig
zig build bench kept alloc_calls=0 after the owner/alias split
application: For future continuation lifecycle changes, make ownership states explicit in the public types before adding runtime guards.

learning: When tightening a documented unsafe Zig API, keep proven-used token names available behind a safer compatibility path so callers get a clear error instead of a silent surface break.
evidence: zig build test failed on the compatibility proof hook until Continuation.clone() and owner release() were restored with tightened semantics
zig build test and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 passed after reintroducing clone/release compatibility in src/control_spec.zig
application: For future safety hardening on exported surfaces, scan docs/examples first and prefer adapters or tightened compatibility methods before removing tokens outright.

learning: When one-shot continuation owners can survive close or destroy as tombstones, keep an explicit non-active owner drain path because removing it entirely leaks handles even if aliasing is otherwise strict.
evidence: zig build test initially leaked in the lifecycle matrix until closed and destroyed owners were explicitly drained via owner release()
zig build test, zig build run-effect-handlers, and zig build bench passed after keeping owner release() while removing clone()
application: For future lifecycle hardening in , treat active-owner duplication and non-active owner drainage as separate API decisions instead of deleting both together.

learning: When a continuation library starts accumulating one-off lifecycle tests, prefer a small reusable law harness because semantic drift becomes easier to catch and future ownership changes stop duplicating setup and drain logic.
evidence: zig build test passed after refactoring lifecycle coverage in src/control_spec.zig through LifecycleLawHarness
zig build run-effect-handlers and zig build bench both passed after promoting EffectSpec and interpreter composition in the public-facing wave
application: For future semantic waves in shift, add new lifecycle or effect laws by extending the shared harness and showcase instead of writing isolated scenario tests.

learning: When linking assembly context-switch stubs through Zig build modules, add the .S file only to the root module that owns the symbol; adding it again to importing example or bench modules produces duplicate symbol failures.
evidence: zig build failed with duplicate symbol definition: _shift_swap_context until build.zig stopped attaching the same aarch64_switch.S file to both the shift module and importing modules
application: Wire low-level assembly once per final artifact and keep example, bench, and test modules dependent on the already-linked library module.

learning: When a Zig direct-style shift/reset handler is meant to model true shift semantics, prefer returning the enclosing Answer and using continuation @"resume" only for the shift-expression Resume, because effect-style handler shapes otherwise trigger ContinuationNotConsumed and semantic drift.
evidence: examples/effect_state.zig initially failed with error.ContinuationNotConsumed until the example was rewritten so the handler returned the enclosing answer while @"resume" supplied the injected value
application: Shape new examples and helper APIs around answer-returning handlers whenever the runtime is claiming shift/reset semantics instead of explicit effect interpretation.

fingerprint.learning: When a nested stackful shift/reset runtime already has one driver loop per reset frame, prefer bubbling captured continuations through parent drivers before rewriting the entire runtime architecture, because that can unlock outer-prompt capture with a much smaller change set.
evidence: src/raw.zig now searches the parent fiber chain for the target prompt and bubbles captures upward; zig build test passes with a new outer prompt capture regression test
fingerprint.application: Before proposing another architectural restart, try routing captured continuations through the existing reset drivers and prove the semantics with a nested-prompt test.

fingerprint.learning: When a stackful control runtime still creates a fresh top-level reset frame per call, prefer treating no-capture performance as a separate architecture problem after semantic fixes land, because the dominant cost remains the top-level context-switch path.
evidence: bench/no_capture_bench.zig output after the outer-prompt fix is iterations=50000 ns=235654958 checksum=1249975000, which is still far from the earlier managed-frame no-capture baseline
fingerprint.application: After semantic fixes, benchmark again and isolate top-level reset entry costs before assuming more control-semantic work will recover the no-capture fast path.

fingerprint.learning: When a stackful shift/reset API needs typed user errors, prefer splitting the public surface into ControlError(ErrorSet) for shift-expression operations and ResetError(ErrorSet) for answer-producing paths, because nested reset calls otherwise force honest setup failures back into anyerror.
evidence: src/root.zig now exports ControlError and ResetError; zig build, zig build test, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, and the example runs all passed after the body and continuation-answer paths were widened to ResetError while shift itself stayed on ControlError
fingerprint.application: For future API tightening in this repo, preserve the control-vs-answer error split instead of collapsing the whole surface back to anyerror.

fingerprint.learning: When a stackful runtime exposes public guard-release or deinit wrappers, prefer adding checked variants that return explicit lifecycle errors before keeping convenience wrappers, because silent cross-thread leave or deinit-during-reset is a misuse footgun.
evidence: src/raw.zig now exposes NoShiftGuard.leaveChecked() and Runtime.deinitChecked(); zig build test passes new misuse regressions for cross-thread guard release and deinit during an active reset
fingerprint.application: For future public lifecycle surfaces in this repo, ship an explicit checked API first and keep any void wrapper as a thin trap-only convenience around it.

fingerprint.learning: When a public stackful runtime exposes checked teardown, prefer giving the runtime an explicit destroyed state and rejecting reuse after successful deinit, because otherwise a second deinit or later reset can walk deinitialized storage.
evidence: src/raw.zig now tracks Runtime.state and zig build test passes a regression that expects error.RuntimeDestroyed on double deinitChecked() and reset() after teardown
fingerprint.application: For future lifecycle APIs in this repo, model terminal runtime states explicitly instead of relying on deallocated containers to fail implicitly.

fingerprint.learning: When prompt identity is only used for internal matching in a stackful shift/reset runtime, prefer a collision-free per-type token derived from a per-instantiation static marker instead of hashing @typeName(Tag), because hashing leaves a correctness risk that deterministic token equality can remove entirely.
evidence: src/raw.zig now uses PromptToken plus promptToken(comptime Tag) based on a per-type marker; zig build test passes new same-type, distinct-type, and cross-namespace token tests
fingerprint.application: For future internal identity keys in this repo, prefer exact token identity over probabilistic hashes whenever the key never needs serialization or stable external representation.

fingerprint.learning: When a stackful reset runtime keeps the continuation frame alive only until reset returns, prefer storing ResetFrame on the caller stack instead of heap-allocating it every call, because no-capture resets otherwise pay avoidable allocator cost on the hottest path.
evidence: src/raw.zig now initializes ResetFrame by value and zig build bench improved from iterations=50000 ns=219534167 checksum=1249975000 to iterations=50000 ns=5525792 checksum=1249975000
fingerprint.application: For future top-level reset optimizations in this repo, remove per-call frame heap allocation before attempting more invasive lazy-promotion or stack-switch changes.

fingerprint.learning: When a repo's default benchmark command is meant to guide performance work, prefer wiring that bench step to a dedicated ReleaseFast build of the target module, because benchmarking through the default debug optimize mode obscures real runtime progress.
evidence: build.zig now gives the bench step a ReleaseFast shift module; zig build bench reports iterations=50000 ns=2293417 checksum=1249975000 after the change versus 219534167 ns before the recent perf work and 5525792 ns after the reset-frame stack move alone
fingerprint.application: For future perf work in this repo, keep correctness gates on the default optimize mode but make benchmark entrypoints explicitly performance-oriented by default.

fingerprint.learning: When defining a lint-clean Zig ResumableSpec DSL, use 'tag' for the prompt marker but TitleCase type aliases like 'Answer', 'ErrorSet', 'ResumeValue', and 'Operation'; lowercase 'resume' collides with the language and the mixed casing satisfies zlinter.
evidence: zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 passed only after switching the new ResumableSpec examples/tests/benches to tag + TitleCase type aliases and avoiding lowercase resume.
fingerprint.application: For future Zig DSLs that expose a comptime spec struct, check zlinter naming classes early and design the declarative field names around them instead of retrofitting after the implementation compiles.

fingerprint.learning: When benchmarking a resumable Zig control engine that allocates continuation cells on suspension, use std.heap.smp_allocator for the benchmark runtime or the result is dominated by page_allocator syscall cost rather than the continuation protocol itself.
evidence: With page_allocator, 'zig build bench-first-suspend' measured about 228141459 ns/50000 while 'zig build bench-direct-first-suspend' was about 5643375 ns/50000; after switching the benchmark runtime allocator to std.heap.smp_allocator, resumable first-suspend dropped to about 936917 ns/50000 and beat the direct reference at about 5643375 ns/50000.
fingerprint.application: For future performance claims around resumable continuation allocation, pin the benchmark allocator explicitly and compare against a same-repo reference benchmark before judging the engine design.

fingerprint.learning: When a control library's product identity is direct-style delimited continuations, remove fast-path sidecar engines that require explicit machine reification, because keeping both surfaces blurs the contract and shifts docs and benchmarks away from the primary semantics.
evidence: After adding ResumableSpec, the repo narrative and benchmark defaults moved toward the resumable path; removing src/resumable_spec.zig and its resumable benches restored a single direct-style surface while zig build/test/lint/examples/bench all stayed green.
fingerprint.application: For future shift/reset roadmap choices, treat performance adapters as subordinate to the direct-style API or drop them entirely instead of letting them become a second product.

fingerprint.learning: When an escaping stackful shift/reset runtime moves reset frames from caller stack storage to heap-backed lifetime, add a runtime-local frame freelist immediately, because correctness-first heap frames can pass semantic tests while still blowing the no-capture benchmark budget.
evidence: The first escaping-core rewrite passed zig build/test/check/examples but regressed zig build bench from a 2373792 ns median baseline to 3862833 ns (+62.7%); after adding cached_frames reuse in src/raw.zig, zig build bench improved to a 2541167 ns median (+7.05%) while zig build bench-first-suspend stayed within budget at 6791667 ns (+28.91%).
fingerprint.application: For future lifetime-expanding runtime rewrites in this repo, measure the hot no-capture path immediately after the correctness pass and add reuse at the owning runtime boundary before chasing smaller micro-optimizations.

fingerprint.learning: When an escaping Zig shift/reset runtime already reuses frame and suspension storage, low-risk hot-path micro-optimizations can still improve the no-capture benchmark while failing to move the first-suspend median in a stable direction, so stop after one or two bounded tries and plan a structural suspension-path rewrite instead of stacking more cache-front tweaks.
evidence: A hot-slot cache attempt worsened no-capture and first-suspend medians and was rolled back. A lower-level init-path shave plus removing a duplicate internal runtime-owner check improved zig build bench from the current escaping baseline 2585416 ns median to 2379375 ns, but zig build bench-first-suspend still landed at 5564542 ns versus the 5355708 ns escaping baseline, so the tree was restored to the last good commit.
fingerprint.application: For the next performance pass in this repo, treat an unchanged or worse first-suspend median after two low-risk runtime tweaks as evidence that the remaining cost is structural, not cache-front incidental overhead.

fingerprint.learning: When a structural fallback rewrites an escaping shift/reset API to a stable session driver but still misses the first-suspend benchmark target, keep the fresh escaping baseline artifact and roll the code back immediately, because at that point the remaining path is no longer an implementation detail tweak but a larger product/semantics decision.
evidence: A fresh bench/baselines/escaping_step_v1.json captured a 5416917 ns bench-first-suspend median on c7d57c2. Phase A's internal suspension split stayed correct but missed the target, and Phase B's Session/Event redesign also stayed correct while landing around a 6584542 ns median for bench-first-suspend, so the code tree was restored and only the evidence artifact was kept.
fingerprint.application: For future shift/reset performance work in this repo, treat a failed stable-session redesign as a stop signal to reconsider the public model or workload assumptions before writing more code.

fingerprint.learning: When a one-shot shift/reset library pivots from runtime-oriented suspension mechanics to builder-facing lifecycle semantics, make cancellation a library-owned terminal branch with an explicit recovery error, because otherwise auto-cancel-on-drop and user-controlled discontinue stay semantically entangled and the public contract remains fuzzy.
evidence: The Outcome/Token rewrite kept explicit discontinue(err), added library-owned cancellation plus CancellationRecovered, and the full gate set stayed green after rewriting README, docs, tests, and examples to the new contract.
fingerprint.application: For future semantic redesigns in this repo, separate user-owned error injection from library-owned cancellation as early as the public API sketch, then enforce the distinction in both runtime errors and examples.

fingerprint.learning: When adding a larger real-world Zig example to a control-flow library, split the runnable entrypoint from an importable helper module and drive both stdout and tests through the same writer-based helper so README output, example behavior, and focused proofs cannot drift.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the new job_workflow example stayed lint-clean and passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build check, zig build run-job-workflow, and zig build docs-sanity after examples/job_workflow/workflow.zig exposed reusable runScenario/runShowcase helpers for test/job_workflow_test.zig.
fingerprint.application: For future showcase examples in this repo, keep the executable thin, move deterministic behavior into an importable helper, and make tests capture the same rendered trace the README documents.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig showcase driver prints token payloads before resolving them, prefer errdefer token.deinit() around the writer call because otherwise a writer failure leaves the suspension unresolved and runtime teardown hits error.RuntimeBusy.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, a focused regression test in test/job_workflow_test.zig reproduced error.RuntimeBusy and leaked frame/suspension allocations after job_workflow.runScenario hit error.WriteFailed; after adding errdefer token.deinit() in examples/job_workflow/workflow.zig, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, and zig build run-job-workflow all passed.
fingerprint.application: For future shift examples and drivers, treat writer/logging failures in token-handling branches as ownership boundaries and drain unresolved tokens before propagating the I/O error.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig showcase example graduates from a transcript-only README snippet, prefer adding a short docs walkthrough that maps each printed branch to the exact control operation because that keeps implementation momentum while making the example usable as an orientation tool.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, adding docs/job_workflow.md plus a single README pointer passed zig build docs-sanity and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 without touching the runtime surface, and zig build run-job-workflow still matched the documented transcript.
fingerprint.application: For future examples in this repo, keep README output concise and move branch-by-branch explanation into docs once the example becomes large enough to hide the control-flow lesson.

fingerprint.learning: When a generic Zig helper around token resume, discontinue, and cancel paths passes tests but fails Debug example executables, prefer narrowing the rollout to the stable subset instead of forcing all example migrations through a toolchain bug.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the shared examples/support/driver.zig helper and its dedicated tests passed zig build test, but migrating generator, effect_state, and effect_handlers onto that helper triggered repeatable Debug executable LLVM failures during zig build run-generator and zig build run-effect-state. The stable rollout kept the helper plus tests and the job_workflow migration, while restoring the three small examples to their manual loops so zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, and zig build docs-sanity all passed.
fingerprint.application: For future generic helper refactors in this repo, treat runnable example executables as a separate proof lane from tests and narrow scope when the helper is semantically sound but the current Zig toolchain is not.

fingerprint.learning: When a generic Zig helper over shift token decisions targets specs with and without user error sets, prefer specializing the decision type on an empty ErrorSet because otherwise Debug example builds can hit toolchain failures even when tests and richer examples pass.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the shared examples/support/driver.zig helper originally passed zig build test but generator and effect_state executable builds failed in Debug. After specializing Decision(Spec) and applyDecision for empty ErrorSet specs, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, and zig build docs-sanity all passed.
fingerprint.application: For future generic helper work in this repo, treat empty user error sets as a distinct compile-time case instead of carrying a dead discontinue branch through all specializations.

fingerprint.learning: When promoting a shared Zig workflow helper into a public API, keep it namespaced and additive, migrate only the workflow-style examples to it, and move teaching examples back to direct token loops so the helper does not silently become the canonical surface.
evidence: Added public helper exports in src/driver.zig and src/root.zig while deleting examples/support/driver.zig.

zig build test, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build docs-sanity, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, and zig build run-job-workflow all passed after the migration.
fingerprint.application: For future ergonomic API additions in continuation-style Zig libraries, treat the low-level contract as canonical and use selective example migration plus direct-output proof to show where the helper truly adds value.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig benchmark is supposed to prove a warmed steady-state path, prefer embedding warm-up plus multi-sample median collection in the benchmark binary and comparing candidate vs target on the same machine, because otherwise cold-start drift can create false regressions or hide real ones.
evidence: The original no-capture benchmark mixed runtime cache fill with the timed loop; repeated invocations drifted sharply before the harness changed to 20000 warmup iterations and 5 internal samples.
Same-machine A/B after the harness change passed the 5% gate: bench median 3222042ns vs 3164125ns (+1.83%), bench-first-suspend median 5481416ns vs 5726125ns (-4.27%).
fingerprint.application: For future perf-proof work in this repo, update the benchmark contract first, then require warmed internal medians plus same-machine A/B before treating a regression as real.

fingerprint.learning: When a delimited-control runtime needs a research-heavy redesign, prefer adding a repo-grounded law set, machine correspondence, and decision dossier before changing the public API, because that turns broad theory into concrete surface decisions while keeping the current proof and benchmark lanes honest.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, adding docs/research_laws.md, docs/research_machine.md, docs/research_decision.md, and updating docs/research.md plus README.md kept zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build docs-sanity, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, zig build bench, and zig build bench-first-suspend all green.
fingerprint.application: For future semantics-heavy waves in this repo, land the law/machine/decision documentation slice first and use it to choose the next API cut instead of redesigning the surface directly from external PL literature.

fingerprint.learning: When a one-shot delimited-control API splits normal pending resolution from delayed escape, implement the split in the runtime first, then migrate the driver, examples, benchmarks, and docs in the same wave, because otherwise the build graph keeps compiling stale public-surface assumptions through install-dependent example and bench steps.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, changing Outcome.token/Token to Outcome.pending/Pending plus EscapedToken across src/raw.zig, src/root.zig, src/driver.zig, examples, benches, and docs kept zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build docs-sanity, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, zig build bench, and zig build bench-first-suspend green after bench/example compile failures exposed stale .token branches.
fingerprint.application: For future public-surface breaks in this repo, cut the semantic core first, then update every proof lane that depends on install/build artifacts before treating the new API as stable.

fingerprint.learning: When a delimited-control API refactor keeps correctness green but leaves one warmed benchmark lane outside the prior guardrail, prefer writing an explicit perf-proof artifact before more tuning, because that preserves decision-quality evidence instead of silently normalizing the miss.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the pending-owner public API cut kept zig build test, zig build docs-sanity, and the example suite green, but a fresh bench/baselines/pending_owner_api_perf_proof_v1.json recorded bench median 2953666ns (-8.33%) and bench-first-suspend median 5942666ns (+8.41%) against bench/baselines/direct_style_v2.json.
fingerprint.application: For future performance-sensitive API cuts in this repo, write a scoped proof artifact whenever one benchmark lane regresses beyond the standing threshold, then use that artifact as the starting point for the next optimization slice.

fingerprint.learning: When a microbenchmark lane is noisy enough that a single warmed invocation can flip a regression verdict, prefer checking in a repeated-invocation capture script and a p50-of-medians proof artifact, because that gives the repo a reproducible source of truth instead of one-off benchmark snapshots.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, adding bench/capture_pending_owner_perf_proof.sh and bench/baselines/pending_owner_api_perf_proof_v2.json showed the pending-owner API cut had bench p50 3062209ns (-4.96%) and bench-first-suspend p50 5459958ns (-0.39%) over 7 warmed invocations, replacing the earlier one-off artifact that had implied a first-suspend regression.
fingerprint.application: For future perf follow-ups in this repo, regenerate the repeated-run proof artifact before accepting or rejecting a regression on the warmed benchmark lanes.

fingerprint.learning: When a benchmark lane is noisy enough that one warmed invocation can invert the pass/fail verdict, prefer retiring the one-off proof artifact in favor of a repeated-run artifact, because future perf decisions should anchor on p50-of-medians rather than a single snapshot.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, bench/baselines/pending_owner_api_perf_proof_v1.json was retired after bench/capture_pending_owner_perf_proof.sh and bench/baselines/pending_owner_api_perf_proof_v2.json showed the pending-owner first-suspend lane at -0.39% over 7 warmed invocations instead of the earlier one-off +8.41% artifact result.
fingerprint.application: For future noisy perf lanes in this repo, regenerate and point docs at repeated-run proof artifacts before accepting a regression narrative.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig API can only make some ownership misuses impossible, prefer hiding unsupported operations from the type surface and proving them with compile-fail fixtures wired into zig build test, because that raises the floor for normal callers while leaving the runtime backstops to cover the cases Zig cannot statically forbid.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, removing discontinue from Pending/EscapedToken when Spec.ErrorSet is empty and wiring test/compile_fail fixtures into build.zig kept zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build compile-fail, zig build test, zig build docs-sanity, the runnable examples, and both benchmark commands green.
fingerprint.application: For future API-hardening work in this repo, encode the strongest feasible restriction in the public type surface first, then add compile-fail fixtures for the now-unrepresentable misuse patterns before relying on runtime errors alone.

fingerprint.learning: When finishing a public terminology migration in shift, prefer renaming the public type and its alias-misuse error together and proving the old surface is gone with a compile-fail fixture plus a repo grep, because mixed old/new naming can survive a green positive-test suite.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, renaming EscapedToken->EscapedOwner and TokenAliased->OwnerAliased stayed green under 'zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0', 'zig build compile-fail', 'zig build test', 'zig build docs-sanity', all four example runs, both bench commands, and 'rg -n "EscapedToken|TokenAliased|token lifecycle" README.md docs src test examples --glob '!test/compile_fail/escaped_token_removed.zig' --glob '!test/compile_fail/run.sh'' returned no matches.
fingerprint.application: For future public rename waves in this repo, pair the symbol rename with its related public error and doc language and add an explicit no-alias proof instead of relying on positive tests alone.

fingerprint.learning: When a shift public API has a resume transition whose payload type is void, prefer specializing the surface to payloadless names like proceed() and .proceed plus compile-fail guards, because forcing resumeWith({}) and .resume_value = {} keeps a meaningless value path alive in docs, examples, and callers.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, specializing Pending/EscapedOwner void resumes to proceed() and shift.driver.Decision(void_spec) to .proceed kept 'zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0', 'zig build compile-fail', 'zig build test', 'zig build docs-sanity', the four runnable examples, and both benchmark commands green after adding compile-fail fixtures for Pending.resumeWith, EscapedOwner.resumeWith, and driver Decision.resume_value on void resume specs.
fingerprint.application: For future public API waves in this repo, derive the surface from whether a machine edge carries data; when it does not, remove the payload-shaped API and lock the specialization with compile-fail and example proof.

fingerprint.learning: When splitting a monolithic Zig runtime file without semantic drift, prefer a thin compatibility barrel over a delete-and-rename move, and prove the split with both the existing closure suite and an explicit public-surface diff audit, because green tests alone do not prove the facade stayed untouched.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, splitting src/raw.zig into src/raw_kernel.zig plus src/raw_surface.zig while keeping src/raw.zig as the barrel stayed green under 'zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0', 'zig build test', 'zig build compile-fail', 'zig build docs-sanity', the four runnable examples, both benchmark commands, and 'git diff --name-only -- src/root.zig src/driver.zig README.md docs examples test/compile_fail test/example_driver_test.zig test/size_check.zig' returned no output.
fingerprint.application: For future internal refactors in this repo, keep the old raw entrypoint stable first, move only the private-internal tests that truly need relocation, and require a no-drift file-surface audit before calling the split safe.

fingerprint.learning: When a warmed benchmark gate is symmetric around a fixed baseline, repeated proof reruns can fail because the candidate is materially faster; prefer confirming multi-run stability before refreshing the tracked proof artifact or declaring closure.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the five-idea wave closure suite passed, but three repeated runs of bench/capture_pending_owner_perf_proof.sh produced no-capture delta_pct values of -4.88, -5.93, and -6.88 while bench_first_suspend stayed within threshold; st-008 remains in_progress with the blocker recorded in .step/st-plan.jsonl.
fingerprint.application: For future shift perf-gated turns, rerun the warmed proof multiple times and treat a symmetric >5% improvement as a policy question or baseline-refresh candidate before overwriting the tracked JSON artifact.

fingerprint.learning: When a delimited-control docs wave is meant to constrain future API stories, add one escaped-owner witness that resumes later and suspends again; it proves re-delimitation more directly than prose alone.
evidence: Added src/raw_surface.zig test 'escaped owner delayed resolution re-delimits resumed work' and then passed zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build compile-fail, zig build docs-sanity, and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 on 2026-03-10.
fingerprint.application: On future shift/reset semantic or documentation waves, anchor the narrative on one executable delayed-escape trace before refining the machine or public API docs.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig repo wants executable semantic claims, prefer a Zig-native witness registry plus transcript-locked runner over docs-only narratives, because it keeps examples, tests, and entrypoints aligned under one source of truth.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift on 2026-03-10, adding src/control_lab/registry.zig, src/control_lab/scenarios.zig, examples/control_studio/main.zig, test/control_studio_test.zig, docs/control_lab.md, and build.zig wiring kept zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build control-studio-check, zig build test, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, zig build run-control-studio -- list, and bench/capture_pending_owner_perf_proof.sh green.
fingerprint.application: On future semantics-heavy waves in this repo, define the witness set once in Zig data, drive a dedicated runner and transcript-lock tests from it, and make docs point to that executable center instead of becoming the center themselves.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig delimited-control API moves from type-tag prompts to per-instance prompt handles, keep Request, Resume, Answer, and ErrorSet as associated types on the handle so the runtime can preserve compile-time specialization while matching delimiters by handle identity.
evidence: shift refocus branch introduced shift.Prompt(Request, Resume, Answer, ErrorSet) plus prompt-instance matching in src/raw_kernel.zig and passed zig build/test/bench closure on 2026-03-11
fingerprint.application: On future handle-based control refactors, move prompt identity to the handle instance without throwing away the type-level request and resume contract.

fingerprint.learning: When a major Zig API reset is blocked by legacy test declarations inside imported modules, prefer executable smoke checks as the first proof lane because they let the new surface land before every embedded test is rewritten.
evidence: The shift refocus branch replaced addTest-based driver/control-lab paths with executable smoke and size checks in build.zig, then regained green closure with zig build/test/size-check/compile-fail/docs-sanity/bench commands on 2026-03-11
fingerprint.application: During large public-surface rewrites, move initial validation to runnable executables or harnesses before cleaning up the full historical test corpus.

fingerprint.learning: When narrowing a Zig continuation library from a broader control runtime to a smaller shipped surface, remove legacy branches from the public outcome and owner types first because that forces examples, benches, and smoke checks onto the real product contract immediately.
evidence: The second shift refocus wave removed public Outcome.cancelled plus public cancel/discontinue-facing handling from the shipped surface and still passed zig build lint/test/size-check/compile-fail/docs-sanity/bench/bench-first-suspend/bench-delayed-escape on 2026-03-11
fingerprint.application: On future surface-narrowing refactors, change the public sum types and owner methods early so stale product assumptions fail loudly in examples and benchmarks.

fingerprint.learning: When a continuation library claims full linearity through the runtime, require escaped-owner deinit to free owned continuation data directly because any hidden cancellation or stack re-entry means the runtime is still not first-order.
evidence: The linear-dsl-vnext wave in shift replaced the live reset/shift stackful surface with src/linear.zig, added run plus first-order Frame/Resume/Suspend machines, and passed zig build, test, size-check, compile-fail, docs-sanity, bench, bench-first-suspend, and bench-delayed-escape on 2026-03-11
fingerprint.application: On future control-runtime rewrites, use direct-free deinit as a hard witness that ownership is truly on the continuation data rather than on hidden runtime machinery.

fingerprint.learning: When a narrowed Zig library keeps an old runtime tree that is no longer referenced by code, tests, or build wiring, remove the archived tree and its last doc pointer once lint and test still pass.
evidence: rg -n 'experimental-control-runtime|archive/' . returned no matches after deleting archive/
zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 -> No issues!
zig build test exited 0 after archive/ removal
fingerprint.application: For future surface contractions, treat git history as the archive and delete dead in-tree runtime copies after proving the live package stands alone.

fingerprint.learning: When rewriting the top of a technical library README for contributor orientation, prefer replacing the old intro block with one named-surface purpose section plus one explicit non-goal sentence, because stacking both versions creates duplicate framing and makes old runtime assumptions linger.
evidence: README.md now flows from # shift to ## Purpose to ## Build to ## Examples with the old intro bullets removed
zig build docs-sanity exited 0 after the README edit
README.md line 13 and docs/api.md line 60 both state that the live surface is not a native-body reset/shift runtime
fingerprint.application: For future README purpose rewrites in shift-like repos, use one top-of-file purpose block with the live exported surfaces and one disambiguating non-goal instead of additive intro prose.

fingerprint.learning: When replacing shift's public control model with a checked effect DSL, prefer flipping codegen, root exports, docs, tests, examples, and benchmark gates in one wave because a half-switched repo leaves runtime-first assumptions behind even when the build is green.
evidence: zig build, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build run-basic-resume, zig build run-workflow, zig build bench, zig build bench-basic-effect, and zig build bench-workflow all passed after the replacement wave.
fingerprint.application: For future shift surface pivots, land the compiler/toolchain and the public-facing docs/examples/tests together instead of staging a half-switched repo.

fingerprint.learning: When hardening a Zig 0.15.2 compiler slice under zlinter, prefer migrating deprecated managed lists to allocator-passing std.ArrayList plus local context structs and errdefer cleanup because that removes doctrine skips without changing generated artifacts.
evidence: zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build check-generated, and zig build test all passed in /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift after removing the temporary no_deprecated, max_positional_args, and require_errdefer_dealloc skips from build.zig.
fingerprint.application: For future shift compiler/tooling cleanup, move allocator ownership and repeated context through small wrappers instead of adding lint exclusions around deprecated std APIs or long helper signatures.

fingerprint.learning: When committing a repo-wide shift surface replacement, prefer staging the full checked DSL, generated artifacts, docs, tests, and learnings together because partial staging leaves the cached proof out of sync with the live product contract.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the checked-DSL replacement and stricter-doctrine follow-up both validated cleanly before commit via zig build, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build check-generated, and the generated examples/benches.
fingerprint.application: For future shift pivots, stage the whole validated surface and re-check cached diff scope before committing or pushing.

fingerprint.learning: When refocusing a delimited-continuation repo back to direct-style shift/reset semantics, prefer restoring the last semantically aligned runtime surface and then deleting competing product directions in one wave because mixed semantic stories survive longer than mixed code paths.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, restoring the 85a6d3d direct-style kernel shape, deleting the generated-surface files, and adding the witness/reference ladder still passed zig build, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build compile-fail, zig build docs-sanity, zig build run-generator, zig build run-early-exit, and zig build run-nested-workflow.
fingerprint.application: For future shift semantic pivots, restore the matching kernel surface first, then remove the obsolete product lane and re-establish witnesses and docs immediately.

fingerprint.learning: When a semantics-first Zig control repo is catching its proof ladder up to a live runtime, prefer adding a manifest that ties each active witness to its law anchor, evaluator case, runtime case, and required or forbidden transcript because that turns semantic drift into a concrete CI failure instead of a review-time intuition.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, adding test/semantic_manifest.zig and deepening src/reference_eval.zig still passed zig build, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build compile-fail, zig build docs-sanity, zig build run-generator, zig build run-early-exit, and zig build run-nested-workflow.
fingerprint.application: For future shift semantic rungs, add the manifest before widening runtime claims so every new witness or law has an explicit proof slot in CI.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig shift/reset API moves from tag-type delimiters to prompt values, prefer migrating runtime identity, witnesses, examples, benches, compile-fail fixtures, and docs in the same wave because splitting the migration leaves the repo telling contradictory delimiter stories.
evidence: src/raw.zig prompt token switched to per-instance prompt values
src/witnesses.zig multi_prompt now proves same-type prompt-instance separation
proof: zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 passed
fingerprint.application: Use one prompt-value migration wave instead of runtime-only incrementalism.

fingerprint.learning: When a semantics-first Zig shift/reset repo already has transcript-locked witnesses and an evaluator, prefer adding an executable reference machine backend and requiring evaluator, machine, and optimized runtime to agree because that catches semantic drift earlier than runtime-versus-docs checks alone.
evidence: src/reference_machine.zig added executable hard-witness machine states
test/witness_corpus_test.zig now compares runtime, evaluator, and reference machine against semantic_manifest
proof: zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build passed
fingerprint.application: Treat the reference machine as a permanent oracle backend once the prompt-value API is stable enough to support three-lane witness checks.

fingerprint.learning: When a CoreSR-Full rewrite branch needs a binary success-or-impossibility ending, prefer adding a real one-shot survey step and a Closure Ledger because that turns branch closure from hand-wavy judgment into auditable evidence.
evidence: build.zig adds a one-shot-survey step
test/one_shot_survey/run.sh seeds compile-success unsoundness probes for alias_copy and store_escape
proof: zig build one-shot-survey && zig build test && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 passed
fingerprint.application: Use a survey harness plus ledger whenever the branch may have to close on a proved impossibility result.

fingerprint.learning: When a rewrite branch still owes ATM-bearing public types, prefer writing the typed CoreSR-Full derivation and an ATM Surface Table before treating one-shot survey results as meaningful because otherwise the branch can declare impossibility against the wrong API.
evidence: docs/core_sr_full.md defines the intended Prompt(InAnswer, OutAnswer, ErrorSet) surface
docs/atm_surface_table.md maps public types to calculus, CPS, and runtime roles
proof: zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build one-shot-survey passed
fingerprint.application: Make derivation-first a concrete artifact checkpoint, not just a planning slogan, whenever ATM is still unsettled.

fingerprint.learning: When a rewrite branch lands an ATM-bearing public type surface before the runtime can execute the non-diagonal case, prefer changing the code surface now and failing non-diagonal execution at compile time because that keeps the branch honest while the semantic and runtime layers catch up.
evidence: src/root.zig and src/raw.zig now use Prompt(InAnswer, OutAnswer, ErrorSet)
test/compile_fail/non_diagonal_atm_unimplemented.zig locks the explicit compile-time stop for non-diagonal execution
proof: zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build docs-sanity && zig build one-shot-survey passed
fingerprint.application: When ATM-bearing public types are ready before ATM execution semantics, land the surface and guard the unsupported path explicitly instead of deferring the entire rewrite.

fingerprint.learning: When an ATM-bearing prompt surface is already landed on a rewrite branch, prefer implementing one concrete non-diagonal witness across evaluator, reference machine, and runtime before broadening the witness family or the one-shot survey because that turns ATM from a type-only story into executable branch truth.
evidence: src/witnesses.zig adds atm_resume_transform
src/reference_eval.zig and src/reference_machine.zig both execute atm_resume_transform
proof: zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build one-shot-survey passed
fingerprint.application: Use one witness-backed non-diagonal path as the first runtime ATM checkpoint once the public type surface is in place.

fingerprint.learning: When a broader ordinary-Zig survey for one-shot CoreSR-Full only reproduces alias-copy and store-escape failures across the planned family set, prefer closing the rewrite branch as IMPOSSIBLE with a memo and ledgers because continued search stops adding new evidence.
evidence: test/one_shot_survey/run.sh now reports compile_success for alias_copy, store_escape, typestate_value, consumed_wrapper, borrowed_token, split_token, opaque_capsule, and comptime_capability
docs/closure_ledger.md records the planned families as fundamental or non-improving failures and triggers the stop rule
proof: zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build docs-sanity && zig build one-shot-survey passed
fingerprint.application: Use the stop rule to end a rewrite branch truthfully once additional plain-Zig families stop producing new failure classes.

fingerprint.learning: When a rewrite branch reaches an evidence-backed IMPOSSIBLE result, prefer committing the truthful branch-local closure package in one shot because splitting the memo, ledgers, survey corpus, and ATM witness state across multiple commits weakens the branch conclusion.
evidence: commit 6651a11 closes rewrite/core-sr-full with docs/impossible_plain_zig.md, docs/closure_ledger.md, docs/atm_witness_ledger.md, and the completed one-shot survey corpus
proof before commit: zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build docs-sanity && zig build one-shot-survey && zig build run-generator passed
fingerprint.application: When a branch ends in IMPOSSIBLE, package the memo, ledgers, survey artifacts, and proof-backed code state together in one closure commit.

fingerprint.learning: When plain Zig cannot enforce one-shot use for a public continuation handle, reopen the seam by moving the raw continuation internal, adding PromptMode-selected comptime handler protocols, and resetting the survey against that narrower surface before concluding impossible again.
evidence: rewrite/core-sr-full now compiles with PromptMode-selected handler protocols and no public shift.Continuation export
zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build docs-sanity && zig build one-shot-survey all pass on the reopened seam
fingerprint.application: For future continuation-library deadlocks, narrow the public eliminator surface before relaxing to a verifier or runtime-only safety story.

fingerprint.learning: When a continuation branch reopens on a narrower seam after an impossible result, prefer closing it as SUCCESS only after semantic witnesses, practical witnesses, and seam-correct rechecks of the old failure classes are all green, because otherwise the branch can inherit a false success from the seam change alone.
evidence: rewrite/core-sr-full now has semantic witnesses for .resume_then_transform and .direct_return, practical green runs for generator/early-exit/nested-workflow, and seam-correct alias/store rechecks in test/one_shot_survey

zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build docs-sanity && zig build size-check && zig build && zig build one-shot-survey && zig build run-generator && zig build run-early-exit && zig build run-nested-workflow all pass
fingerprint.application: When closing future seam-rewrite branches, require seam-correct survey evidence and practical witness coverage before flipping docs or memory from reopened to success.

fingerprint.learning: When branch closure claims practical witness coverage, prefer locking each practical witness directly in the test corpus rather than inferring coverage from one example plus docs, because otherwise the success story can drift ahead of the executable proof.
evidence: rewrite/core-sr-full claimed generator, early-exit, and nested-workflow were green while test/witness_corpus_test.zig only locked generator before this fix

zig build test && zig build compile-fail && zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 && zig build docs-sanity && zig build one-shot-survey && zig build run-generator && zig build run-early-exit && zig build run-nested-workflow all pass after adding locked early-exit and nested-workflow practical tests
fingerprint.application: When a branch closure depends on a practical trio, add explicit locked tests for each member instead of relying on example commands or narrative closure docs.
